era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt under Thutmose III. The New Kingdom was at its height of territorial expansion and cultural achievement. The Mitanni kingdom controlled northern Mesopotamia and Syria. The Hittite Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia. Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period. This era is characterized by intensive military campaigns, administrative consolidation, and the development of international trade networks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Shang Dynasty records exist but are sparse and chronologically uncertain)

events.title: Thutmose III's military campaigns in the Levant
events.description: Thutmose III continued his systematic military campaigns to consolidate Egyptian control over Levantine territories. These campaigns were part of a long-term strategy to establish Egyptian hegemony and secure tribute networks. The campaigns are documented in temple inscriptions and administrative records.

sources.name: Karnak Temple inscriptions (Annals of Thutmose III)

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's reign is well-attested, but specific year-by-year events are difficult to pinpoint. This entry reflects the general pattern of his campaigns.

events.title: Mycenaean Greece in Late Helladic II period
events.description: Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period, characterized by the development of palace complexes, linear B writing, and extensive trade networks. The Mycenaean civilization was expanding its influence throughout the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean. Archaeological evidence shows increasing contact with Egypt and the Levant.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenaean sites (pottery, architecture, Linear B tablets)

events.certainty_note: The Late Helladic II period is well-attested archaeologically; specific year-by-year events are not individually documented.

disconfirming_evidence: As with previous years, the chronology of this period is contested. The 'low chronology' would shift these events significantly later. No specific events are individually attested to 1409 BCE in any contemporary source. The entries above represent the general historical context rather than year-specific documentation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1409 BCE is similar to previous years: dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions and cuneiform records, with significant chronological uncertainty. For Mycenaean Greece, the record is primarily archaeological; Linear B tablets provide some administrative information but are rarely dated to specific years. The surviving sources are biased toward elite political and military history. Archaeological evidence provides some constraints but cannot pinpoint individual years.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same campaign sequence
Mycenaean Greece was contemporaneous with Egyptian campaigns in the Levant
